Later, free mixing and matching will be a thing.,

author: BRUNN
content:
About the Economic Forum… Okay, I think the restructuring is great, but I have some important points to raise.

First: selling ships does make money. We see this in Star Citizen, and recently in Elite Dangerous, which returned to profitability (it’s in Frontier’s investor report). However, both are already well-established games with solid gameplay.
Star Citizen goes for insane levels of detail, while Elite Dangerous is the only space sim with a 1:1 galaxy simulation. And in this landscape, I don’t currently see a truly clear differentiator for Star Atlas.

Yes, we have an economic angle, similar to EVE, but that alone doesn’t seem enough to guarantee its place among current space sims. On top of that, many players don’t want to deal with crypto, and the simple fact that they can’t use a credit card already filters out a massive audience.

Based on that, here are some proposals:

Games that depend on “crypto players” are doomed,
We’ve already seen that this crowd only wants to farm the game until it dries out. That’s not a sustainable player base.

Fix the onboarding,
– Account creation via email, nothing else.
– When the account is created, the system automatically generates a wallet, with the option to export private keys if the user wants to.
– Anyone who doesn’t understand crypto should never be forced to learn it: just email + password.
– Just like Elite sells ARX for real money, players could buy ATLAS or USDC directly from an ATMTA pool using a credit card.
– The tokens automatically go to the wallet tied to the email.
– Login into the game like any normal game: email + password.
Simple, fast, familiar, zero friction.

Focus on building something truly unique,
Competing head-to-head with Star Citizen is madness — it’s impossible.
FPS combat is also a hyper-competitive loop. I would stop investing in that entirely.

The racing loop is already fun and well-developed — I would finish and polish it. After that, I’d target a gap that other space sims are not exploring right now, such as:

• A “realistic No Man’s Sky”

NMS is a huge success, but many players dislike the cartoony visuals.
Right now, there is no realistic space game with procedural exploration of planets full of flora/fauna + survival elements.
Starfield got close, but the exploration aspect clearly lacked refinement.
Yes, it requires tons of assets, but the market gap exists.

• Truly good ship combat

Star Citizen has a ridiculously low TTK — ship combat feels like rock/paper/scissors.
Elite Dangerous has phenomenal combat, but it was never built for proper PvP.
There’s definitely room to innovate here.

• A robust alien-creature hunting system

Something like a mix of theHunter: Call of the Wild (simulation) and Monster Hunter (epic boss encounters).
This is a niche that no other space game is exploring at the moment.

These are the differentiators where I believe Star Atlas could truly shine. I’d focus 100% on one of them to secure a meaningful market share.

But without frictionless onboarding, none of that will be enough.

author: bunthius [STAR]
content:
The first mega release we identified with product market fit was the alien big game hunting. And we're still on track for that. However, as a crowd funded game there are unique challenges where the community needs content along the way. It's impossible for us to say just trust us and wait years for a release. So, to satisfy that demand, as we build functionality and features we create a release around it to deliver content and receive feedback along the way:

character movement - showroom,summoning sft ships - showroom,hover and flight mode - ground and air racing,ship combat - combat racing,weapons - arena shooter,planet tech - galia,multiplayer - showroom, gunplay/ship play,game pacing - galia,
As far as Star Citizen, the only thing I've referenced in relation to Star Atlas is that there are magical moments buried in Star Citizen that are difficult to access due to setup and engagement times. My goal has always been to find those moments and make them accessible to more types of players. It's a similar endeavor where good marvel movies started coming out and now a mainstream audience could see why so many comic book nerds loved the stories and action in them. Or how GTA is not a car sim but it makes a bunch of types of realistic looking vehicles fun to play with. So, I intend to make those awesome things about SC accessible even on a gamepad. 

I'd say I'm basically aligned with it and thought I've always made that stuff clear. It's just a matter of time until these things are viable, but they do take time and crowdfunded games create a unique challenge that gamers aren't used to getting access to. 

Also, we don't even need to have a login or wallet, we can just have a play button and the wallet appears in the background and those who want to continue playing/earning or move their account then they would understand that process more. That's already intended, nothing has changed since the start. Just adaptations to new demands from the community, market or otherwise.

content:
Hey team! I was thinking that, given the high level of complexity C4 will bring with it, it might be desirable for players to face as few obstacles as possible. One simplification we could make is translating the text into multiple languages to increase accessibility at minimal cost.

I realize this would definitely be additional work, but we could find a community-driven solution (see importing a custom JSON file like in SES): the game would use English as the default language (if any custom JSON file is given), while DACs/guilds could download that file, translate it into their own language, and offer it to their members. That way ATMTA doesn’t have to spend time translating anything (at least until Fleet Command arrives, in case), the game becomes more accessible, and the trust system is already in place since responsibility is delegated to the guild.

In this way, the tool is already INSIDE the game, but the data (translations) are provided by the community or even self-produced, since LLMs are basically public domain now, just ask for a translation of the downloaded JSON file and everyone can make their own!

And for today as well, I’ve written my wall of text!

author: Jindo | Star Atlas Mod [STAR]
content:
How will balance changes work for mainnet assets in the future? 

We had an interesting conversation in the holosim chat this morning about the recent balance changes to Holosim assets. Most users understand that holosim does not involve user owned assets and that balance changes will most likely happen frequently within holosim. However, @fibonacci brought up a great point.

How will needed balance changes on assets happen for user owned assets in the future. 
A person buys a ship and its op, that's why they paid the price for it they did,It gets nerfed,They feel like they got the short end of the stick,
I'm a long time gamer, 100% understand balance changes are needed in any game to keep it flowing. The question is, how will those changes be implemented in C4/SAGE? Will they be implemented or will a ship a user buys stay the same indefinitely? If there will be changes, how will those changes be presented to the users? 

When users pay a price for a ship, they expect to get what they paid for. Balance changes are easier in a game where the user is not paying for his assets. @fibonacci is curious how this will be handled for future Star Atlas assets.

author: ROME | Winston [ROME]
content:
Just caught up with the townhall. The single-player mode actually looks really fun, can’t wait to explore it. This feels like the kind of gameplay that truly showcases the lore and lets us immerse ourselves in the universe.

Planetside 2 is starting to show its age, and there really isn’t a sci-fi MMOFPS out there that properly combines vehicles and infantry. This feels like a real opportunity for SA to stand apart and carve out its own space.

Please sell tanks when it comes out.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I bring this up because it’s EVEs model - granted they’re not dealing with crypto.  yes, broker fees are the cost of doing business. IMO it discourages people from listing wildly outside of fair market value at the time. If you list too high or low and your order isn’t filled, it was probably because your order wasn’t extractive enough given the market conditions. IMO it should help narrow the spreads in markets but not discourage overall market activity, when i was referring to the .01 ing - I meant when bots (or players) immediately relist at .01 above or below the market orders. This obviously happens quite frequently - by inducing cost into that mechanism, you introduce a cost trade off in the form of market pvp. Do I continually delist and relist at .01 above and below…maybe? Depends on market pvp mechanics and strategy., As far as the .000000000001 otc orders go. Isn’t that a function of just limiting the number of decimal places you can list an order for? Yes the broker fees don’t inherently solve that, but the otc deals are a matter of current technical design/flaw and less a matter of taxes, fees, and Econ mechanics

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content: Regardless of whether Krigg's issue is the right solution or not, marketplace bots are a huge problem and some actual serious thought needs to be given on how to combat them.   They are one of the major reason for the massive ship price drops.   Look at Treearrows.  They should be around 15k but the bot drove prices down to 9.4k. And they can afford this because they purchased the tree arrow from a scammer for 4k.   One could say the bots are just finding the right price, but with humans this might occur over the course of a week.  With bots it happens in <10 minutes.     The bots can and will also purposely manipulate the market by driving prices down and then disappearing from the market. E.g. Busan Maidenheart market.  I'm not listing in several markets right now because of the bots.  They depress the price.  It's better to NOT list and simply do OTC so bot doesn't drive prices down.  And, players hate them. And these players actively avoid the marketplace because of them.  Shouldn't that be enough to take action?  Providing "liquidity" no longer exists.  Bots just provide exit liquidity for hackers and scammers.

author: The Only SignalONI
content: Just a thought, if a race starts and you pass the first checkpoint and log out it should auto resolve as last place with 0XP.  Also the Busan Pulse doesn’t work as a racer it bugs out the physics are all off with it (mainly just if anything touches it, it’s over it glitches up walls or instantly wrecks so hard your chances in the race are over it’s not viably playable ATM).  Airbike feels amazing and is quickly becoming a meta ship which is great considering how cheap it is and how often it’s available for free.  Always having a free racer option is MASSIVE, onboarding for me was very easy.  Download game, it made my hot wallet for me, I setup the wallet it gave me in Solflare to add ships.  Played maybe 12 hours, racing on Exinade is awesome I get why @bunthius felt bad it wasn’t ready for the Galia Cup because it would have been an awesome promo and holy cow is the difference night and day.  It’s huge that I didn’t have to add SOL to play, it made a wallet for me, etc.  Getting the bonus items and XP and ink helps you feel like you are progressing which is great.  Now that you have these really pretty maps I might suggest tucking the unfinished ones away somewhere because there’s enough going on now that a user can look around and see A LOT of pretty well polished product.  Don’t want them accidentally encountering things in other states and building thoughts on that.  Feels REALLY CLOSE to having several shippable gaming loops.  Maybe add an instance type that auto changes the track at random so people can sit in one instance and still experience multiple tracks without leaving.  The two polished tracks you have are great.  If I added anything at this point maybe some environmental effects like you crash into the trees etc. and stuff actually gets knocked over or stuff reacts from a missed laser blast, or weather and nighttime modes etc.  Lots of people seem to still have a need for speed, maybe could be solved by slightly decreasing boost regen times so people can floor it more.  Demand is higher than I’ve ever seen it in my years here you can logon at random and see 6-10 people playing in instances whenever you do (like 1% of your community online 24/7 is pretty awesome especially considering access to hardware for UE5).  You are turning a corner in UE5 and several people are calling it out, this feels like an actual game to grind and master now in UE5.  Also sounds like siege is inspiring cross faction UE5 battles like MUD v ONI 8v8.  Just need lots of good footage and eyes on this stuff and I bet you get a nice player base boost.  Ah!  And finally obviously dedicated servers will be a big deal really fast (high quality problem), the player servers get buggy at 8+ players and racing is routinely seeing that now.  This is a good thing though you should want an excuse to setup dedicated hosting.  Thanks again guys!
